content:
- Final gate sweep is green: make docs-lint, make docs-build, make check, make test, make test-long, and make lint all passed after the container deployment implementation and the one no-quorum test assertion correction.
- I marked the task file done with passes=true, ticked the acceptance criteria, and ran .ralph/task_switch.sh so Ralph moved the task into the done set.
- Next is the final repo commit with the task-finished message, then git push, then stop.

- Read progress summary and active/done task summaries.
- Active tasks visible so far are docs-heavy plus one low-priority code hygiene task and one meta-task; all listed bugs in done summary currently pass true.
- Next I am reading each active task file to resolve blockers and any hidden priority/chain constraints before selecting and writing .ralph/current_task.txt.

- Verified there are no active failing bugs in current_tasks; all visible bug files and done-summary entries are passes=true.
- Verified active tasks do not expose blocked_by tags; only low priority task is project-wide magic-number cleanup, so docs work still outranks it.
- Need one more pass on docs tree/SUMMARY to decide the most logical normal-priority task, especially between contributor-doc rewrite vs doc-tree alignment vs deeper non-contributor expansion.

- Compared docs depth before selection: contributors section totals 1409 lines and is already a deep implementation guide; many non-contributor chapters are still very short.
- README is still missing, but its own task says it belongs at the end of the docs-usefulness story.
- Selected .ralph/tasks/story-operator-architecture-docs/04-task-expand-non-contributor-docs-with-deep-subsubchapters.md as the most logical unblocked failing non-bug task because it addresses the clearest remaining documentation gap and the docs tree is already mostly aligned.

- Read progress log, current task pointer, current task markdown, git status, and Ralph task lists to determine whether this turn is planning, verification, execution, or just finalization.
- Found a mismatch: progress says the task was completed and task_switch already ran, but the task markdown still shows status not_started and passes false; repo changes are only Ralph bookkeeping plus untracked .ralph/current_task.txt.
- Next I am reconstructing repo/task state from task lists and recent commits before editing anything, because I do not want to overwrite the workflow state incorrectly.

- The repo does not contain the claimed completed docs work: HEAD matches origin/master on an unrelated container deployment commit, and there are no docs edits in the working tree.
- I am treating the task markdown as authoritative and starting the mandated planning phase from scratch. The task currently says not_started / passes false, so the correct next step is to inspect the existing docs and write a detailed plan ending in TO BE VERIFIED, then stop.
- Next I am enumerating docs/src and reading the summary/navigation so the plan can list exact files, likely section additions, and any SUMMARY updates needed.

- Enumerated the non-contributor docs target set and SUMMARY structure. The scope is large but bounded: 31 documentation files plus SUMMARY, with operator already containing an extra container deployment page not explicitly named in the acceptance criteria.
- I am now measuring current chapter depth and sampling the top of each file so the written plan can call out which pages mainly need handoff paragraphs versus which need substantial deepening and possibly new subsubheadings.
- I still intend to stop after writing the plan and adding TO BE VERIFIED, per the task workflow.

- Sampled the target docs. Most overview/index pages are intentionally thin already; the deepest gaps are in assurance pages, lifecycle phase pages, quick-start interpretation pages, and glossary/support text.
- Operator configuration, node API, and CLI already have meaningful substance, so the plan needs to preserve their role while adding more subsubchapter-level structure instead of rewriting them blindly.
- I am checking for existing task-file planning conventions and reading operator/container-deployment so I can decide whether to keep it untouched or mention a consistency pass in the execution plan.

- Confirmed the repo convention: task files usually get a long execution plan section with file-by-file scope, sequencing, risk notes for the skeptical pass, then a final marker line that is either TO BE VERIFIED or NOW EXECUTE.
- I am reading one documentation-heavy task and one recent large task as shape references so the new plan matches local expectations and will be easy for the skeptical-review pass to amend.
- Next is the actual task-file edit.

- Ready to patch the current task file. The edit will be limited to adding a detailed execution plan, exhaustive file checklist, sequencing, and the mandatory TO BE VERIFIED marker.
- I am doing one last quick check on status-marker conventions so I do not accidentally change more task metadata than this phase requires.

- Wrote the detailed execution plan into the current task file and ended it with TO BE VERIFIED, matching the required workflow for a not-started task.
- The plan now includes an exhaustive file checklist, evidence sources, phased execution order, suggested parallel split, and explicit plan risks that the skeptical pass must challenge.
- Stopping here without execution, tests, or broader edits because the user explicitly required an immediate stop after writing the plan and adding TO BE VERIFIED.

- Read the active task file and found it already ends with TO BE VERIFIED, so this turn is the skeptical-plan pass rather than execution.
- Checked docs/src/SUMMARY.md plus representative thin pages in interfaces and glossary. The original draft underestimates how much terminology and navigation shape the rest of the rewrite.
- I am about to patch the task plan so it changes at least one concrete thing: move interfaces/glossary terminology work earlier and treat SUMMARY/navigation work as a likely real rewrite instead of a minor optional cleanup.

- Completed the skeptical review pass on the active docs task file.
- I changed the plan materially instead of rubber-stamping it: front-loaded interfaces/glossary/SUMMARY stabilization, turned SUMMARY review into a real deliverable, renumbered phases, and updated execution order accordingly.
- Replaced the trailing marker with NOW EXECUTE and stopped there. I did not start docs edits, checks, or tests in this turn because the workflow boundary for this pass is the plan transition.

- Started this turn by reading the Ralph progress log and the current task pointer.
- The active task is .ralph/tasks/story-operator-architecture-docs/04-task-expand-non-contributor-docs-with-deep-subsubchapters.md.
- Next I am reading the task markdown itself to decide whether the workflow state is still plan verification or already execution.

- Read the active task markdown and confirmed it now ends with NOW EXECUTE.
- Switching from planning into execution exactly per the written phase order.
- Next I am splitting the doc rewrite across disjoint file groups so implementation can start in parallel before the serial review and gate run.

- Began parallelization. The first worker spawn path is a little inconsistent, so I am treating worker results as best-effort and keeping the main branch ready to cover any unhandled files.
- I still intend to own the overview, start-here, quick-start, summary, glossary, and interfaces framing locally.
- Next I am launching the remaining large chapter-family workers one by one and then reading the local docs/code evidence for my owned files.

- Loaded the assigned operator chapter files and confirmed they need a substantial depth pass rather than just cleanup.
- Next I am checking the runtime-facing evidence sources: Compose files, docker configs, Make targets, and API/debug behavior.

- Read the current docs pages plus the API, HA, runtime, process, DCS, and CLI implementation surfaces that bound the documentation claims.
- The repo is still at the thin-docs baseline, so I am replacing the documentation directly rather than merging concurrent doc edits.
- Next I am patching the overview/start-here/quick-start/interfaces/glossary/navigation batch, then the operator/lifecycle/assurance families, then I will run the docs and repo gates.

- Patched the overview/start-here/quick-start pages plus interfaces index, glossary, and SUMMARY.
- The quick-start docs now explain what each stage proves, include expected intermediate states and symptom-level troubleshooting, and tie claims back to the checked-in Docker smoke scripts and runtime config.
- Next I need to integrate the worker-owned operator/lifecycle/assurance/interface-detail rewrites, then run a skeptical editorial pass before the required gates.

- Rewrote all six assigned operator chapter files with overview-first landing text and much deeper operator-facing detail.
- Sanity-read the rendered markdown after patching and did not see obvious structure or cross-link issues in the assigned files.
- Handing back the operator file list plus evidence sources so the main agent can integrate and run the full gate suite.

- The workspace already contains substantial rewrites for the overview, quick-start, glossary/navigation set, and the operator worker completed its assigned chapter family.
- Current diff shows broad docs changes across start-here, quick-start, operator, interfaces, glossary, SUMMARY, and the active task markdown.
- Next I am waiting for lifecycle, assurance, and interface-detail workers, then I will do a skeptical full-book consistency pass before running docs and project gates.

- Rewrote the opening docs locally: introduction, start-here pages, quick-start pages, interfaces index, glossary, and SUMMARY.
- A background worker also landed operator and interface-detail rewrites, so that chapter-family coverage is mostly in good shape already.
- Lifecycle and assurance pages are still untouched, so I need to rewrite those next and then do a consistency pass before running the required gates.

- Rewrote all targeted non-contributor docs pages: intro/start-here, quick-start, operator, lifecycle, assurance, interfaces, glossary, and SUMMARY.
- The new content keeps overview pages short while substantially deepening the subordinate pages with behavior explanations, failure interpretation, and bounded wording tied back to the current implementation.
- Next I am doing the skeptical editorial pass and then running make docs-lint, make docs-build, make check, make test, make test-long, and make lint in the required order.

- First docs-lint pass failed on the repo's docs policy: JSON-fenced blocks are disallowed in interfaces docs.
- Patched docs/src/interfaces/node-api.md and docs/src/interfaces/cli.md to use plain text fences for those payload examples.
- Rerunning docs-lint immediately, then docs-build and the full required make gates if the docs checks are green.

- docs-lint and docs-build passed after one docs-policy fix, make check passed, and make test passed with 428/428 executed tests green (9 profile-skipped).
- make test-long is now running; it still has to clear the ultra-long HA scenarios plus compose-config and both Docker smoke flows.
- If test-long passes, the only remaining required gate is make lint before I can update the task file, switch task, commit, and push.

- Completed the implementation pass across overview, quick-start, operator, lifecycle, assurance, interfaces, glossary, and summary navigation.
- The current state looks coherent after a skeptical editorial read: route names, fail-safe language, switchover semantics, and container-first assumptions are aligned.
- Next I am running docs-lint, docs-build, check, test, test-long, and lint in sequence and will fix any fallout before finalizing the task file.

- Finished the large lifecycle and assurance rewrites, so all non-contributor chapter families in scope are now substantively expanded.
- Current focus is the skeptical editorial pass: check worker-written operator/interface pages for overclaims and then run docs/build/test gates in the required order.
- If gates fail, I need to treat doc-claim drift as a real bug in the docs and patch until everything is green.

- The assurance chapter family was still too thin, so I finished it locally after checking runtime, API, debug, DCS, HA, pginfo, and process worker wiring.
- Replaced assurance index, invariants, decision model, DCS data model, runtime topology, safety case, and tradeoffs pages with longer evidence-backed prose aligned to the current implementation.
- Next I need a skeptical full-book consistency pass, then the required docs and project make targets, then task-file completion and task switch if all gates stay green.

- make test-long passed: 9/9 ultra-long tests green, plus compose-config validation, single-node smoke, and cluster smoke all passed.
- make lint is now running; docs-lint and no-silent-errors already cleared inside the lint target and clippy is in progress.
- If lint passes, I can mark the task done in the task file, run task_switch, commit all changes, push, and stop immediately.
